abstract:
Recent progress in generative artificial intelligence has fundamentally transformed image synthesis, yet the application of these techniques to three-dimensional (3D) medical imaging remains largely unexplored. While diffusion probabilistic models have demonstrated superior performance in generating realistic 2D images, medical diagnosis relies heavily on volumetric data from modalities such as computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Systematic evaluation of 3D diffusion models is essential, as synthetic data offers a promising solution for privacy-preserving AI development and data augmentation in rare-disease research.

In this study, we introduce a latent diffusion model architecture specifically designed for 3D medical image generation. By operating within the compressed latent space of a Vector Quantized Generative Adversarial Network (VQ-GAN), our approach significantly reduces the computational burden associated with 3D data while maintaining high-fidelity reconstruction. We validated the model across four diverse datasets, including brain MRI, chest CT, breast MRI, and knee MRI. Quantitative and qualitative assessments were conducted, including a reader study where two experienced radiologists evaluated the synthetic volumes for anatomical accuracy, image realism, and cross-slice consistency. 

Our results demonstrate that the proposed diffusion models produce high-quality, diverse 3D samples and are more robust against mode collapse compared to traditional GAN-based baselines. Furthermore, we illustrate a practical clinical application: using synthetic images for self-supervised pre-training significantly enhances the performance of downstream segmentation tasks in low-data regimes, improving breast MRI segmentation Dice scores from 0.91 to 0.95. These findings suggest that latent diffusion models can effectively synthesize complex medical volumes, providing a powerful tool for advancing medical AI research where data scarcity and privacy are primary concerns.
